program.title: Namma Kannada
program.subtitle: Learn Bangalore Kannada the way it is actually spoken

stages.name: Grammar Shapes
stages.description: Before learning vocabulary, learn how Kannada sentences are built. Word order is reversed from English. Yes and no work differently. Every pattern here will carry you through the next three stages.

sentences.grammar_note: beku ends the sentence. Always. Kannada is verb-final — the action comes last.
beku and beda are a pair. beku = yes I want. beda = no I don't.
ide means something exists. Not 'is' as in identity — as in presence. Coffee ide = coffee is available.
ide and illa are the most useful pair in Kannada. Learn them as one unit.
haudu is a full sentence. You don't need anything else to say yes.
sari is the most common word in Bangalore Kannada. Agreement, acknowledgement, moving on — all covered.
aaytu closes things. Meeting over: aaytu. Food ordered: aaytu. Understood: aaytu.
Stick alvaa at the end of anything to make it a tag question. Like 'innit' in British English.
Subject goes first. Then object. Then verb. Naanu coffee beku = I coffee want.
Rising intonation makes it a question. neevu is polite you — use with strangers, elders, anyone you'd call sir or ma'am.
enu is what. adu is that. Adu enu = that what = what is that. Question word comes at the end.
idu = this. adu = that. idu enu? = what is this?
elli = where. Combine with ide or illa: elli ide? = where is it? elli illa? = where is it not? (it's gone)
eshtu alone is a complete question. Price, quantity, distance, time — it covers all of them.
No verb needed for a price question. Coffee eshtu? = Coffee how much?
gottilla is your most useful escape hatch. Also: gottu = I know / understood.
Swap any noun: mane elli ide? = where is home? darshini elli ide? = where is the restaurant?
-ge attaches to the destination. mane-ge = to home. market-ge = to market. No space before it.
banni is come (polite). baa is come (casual/intimate). Use banni with anyone you don't know well.

sentences.gloss: What is there at the office?
sentences.grammar_note: -alli means in or at. mane-alli = at home. bus-alli = in the bus. -ge is movement, -alli is location.

sentences.gloss: There is a lot of traffic
sentences.grammar_note: tumbaa is your intensity word. tumbaa ide = a lot is there. tumbaa beda = way too much, don't want.

sentences.new_word_meaning: a little / slightly / just a bit
sentences.grammar_note: svalpa and tumbaa are your quantity pair. svalpa beku = just a little. tumbaa beda = not so much.
Time words go first, before the subject. Ivattu naale eega — today tomorrow now — always at the front.
barteeni = I will come / I am coming. The -teeni ending means I (first person, future/present continuous).
maadteeri = you are doing (polite). maadteeni = I am doing. Swap -teeni (I) / -teeri (you polite).
alla is emphatic not. illa = does not exist. alla = is not the case. adu sari alla = that is not correct.
aadre is the pivot word. Adu sari alla — aadre enu maadodu? = That is not right — but what to do?
English nouns slot straight in. phone-ge call beku = phone-to call want. The Kannada grammar holds, English words fill the slots.

sentences.gloss: Right, it has been a long time
sentences.grammar_note: tumbaa aaytu = a lot has happened / it's been a while. Literally: a lot has become.
The -ona ending means let us. hogona = let us go. maataadona = let us talk. A gentle way to close a conversation.

stages.name: Daily Survival
stages.description: The language of Bangalore daily life — commute, food, office, phone. Most nouns are still English but the grammar and verbs are Kannada. This is how newcomers to the city actually speak.

sentences.grammar_note: -teeni is the first-person present-continuous ending. hogteeni = I am going. barteeni = I am coming. maadteeni = I am doing.
-teeri is the polite second-person ending. hogteeni = I am going. hogteeri = you are going (polite). Swap the ending to change the person.
baa = come (command). barteeni = I am coming. barteeri = you are coming. The verb root bar- carries the meaning, the ending carries the person.
yaavaaga is when. It goes at the start of the question. yaavaaga barteeri = when are you coming? yaavaaga hogteeri = when are you going?
-alli on a vehicle means by that vehicle. bus-alli = by bus. metro-alli = by metro. auto-alli = by auto. Same postposition, different meaning from location.
-ona is the let-us ending. hogona = let us go. kudiyona = let us drink. maataadona = let us talk. Works on any verb.

sentences.new_word_meaning: will get / will be found / will be available
sentences.grammar_note: sigatte = will be found / available. auto sigatte = auto available. room sigatte = room available. signal sigatte = signal will come.

sentences.new_word_meaning: will not be found / not available
sentences.grammar_note: sigatte and sigalla are the availability pair. sigatte = will find. sigalla = won't find. Works for anything you are looking for.

sentences.gloss: Traffic is a lot, will be late
sentences.grammar_note: aagatte = it will happen. late aagatte = will be late. ready aagatte = will be ready. It is the general future tense word.

sentences.gloss: There is a little time, come quickly
sentences.grammar_note: bega = quickly or soon. bega banni = come quickly. bega hogteeni = going fast. bega aagatte = will happen soon.

sentences.gloss: Is food done? / Have you eaten?
sentences.grammar_note: aaytu = done. oota aaytu = food is done / eating is done. Adding -aa at the end makes it a question. Short and completely natural.
maadu = do / make. maadteeni = I am doing. oota maadteeni = I am eating (literally: doing food). kelasa maadteeni = I am working.
In food orders, number comes after the item. idli eradu = two idlis. coffee ondu = one coffee. dosa mooru = three dosas. Then beku at the end.
maadi is the polite command form of maadu. kammi maadi = make it less. jaasti maadi = make it more. Any adjective + maadi = a food customization request.
Repeating an adjective intensifies it. bisi = hot. bisi bisi = very hot. tumbaa tumbaa = extremely a lot. This doubling pattern is natural in Kannada.

sentences.gloss: How much did the bill come to?
sentences.grammar_note: eshtu aaytu = how much has it become. Works for price, time, quantity. eshtu aaytu = how much total. eshtu gamte aaytu = how many hours has it been.
neeru is one of the first words you need anywhere. svalpa neeru beku = need a little water. cold water beku = need cold water. Direct and sufficient.
darshini is the Bangalore word for a simple, clean vegetarian restaurant. Not hotel (which means any restaurant). Asking for a darshini signals you know the city.
English adjective + aaytu works naturally. ready aaytu = is ready. full aaytu = it is full. late aaytu = it got late. Aaytu is a workhorse.
chennaagide = is good (present). chennaagittu = was good (past). Swap -ide for -ittu to shift to past tense. Works on most descriptors.
elli ide is your location question template. Replace any noun: auto elli ide? wifi elli ide? washroom elli ide? Always works.
Same template, swap elli for yaavaaga. elli = where. yaavaaga = when. elli ide = where is it. yaavaaga ide = when is it.

sentences.new_word_meaning: I am (at a place) / I am here
sentences.grammar_note: iddini = I am here / I am present. Different from ide (it exists). iddini = I exist here. aaphees-alli iddini = I am at the office. mane-alli iddini = I am at home.

sentences.gloss: I will call / I am calling
sentences.grammar_note: English noun + maadteeni = doing that thing. call maadteeni = calling. work maadteeni = working. note maadteeni = taking notes. Highly productive pattern.

sentences.gloss: Can I make a call? / Is it ok to call?
sentences.new_word_meaning: can do / it is possible to do
sentences.grammar_note: maadabohudu = can be done / is permissible. hogubohuduaa? = can I go? koorabohuduaa? = can I sit? Adding -aa makes it a polite request.
Any destination + -ge hogona = let us go to that place. market-ge hogona. mane-ge hogona. coffee-ge hogona (let us go for coffee).

sentences.gloss: Meeting is done, I am going home
sentences.grammar_note: Two clauses joined by a pause, no conjunction needed. X aaytu, Y hogteeni = X is done, going to Y. This is how Kannadigas structure multi-part sentences in casual speech.
Time word + ide is the simplest way to state a schedule. meeting naale ide. holiday ivattu ide. delivery aamele ide.
Any English abbreviation slots into maadteeni. WFH maadteeni. OT maadteeni (doing overtime). PR maadteeni (raising a PR).
English adjective + iddini = I am that way. busy iddini. tired iddini. free iddini (I am free / available). tumbaa busy iddini = I am very busy.
eshtu gamte = how many hours = what time. eega eshtu gamte = what time is it now? mooru gamte aaytu = it has become 3 o'clock.
number + nimisha aagatte = will take that many minutes. eshtu nimisha aagatte? = how many minutes will it take? eradu gamte aagatte = will take two hours.

sentences.gloss: When will you come in the morning?
sentences.grammar_note: beligge = morning. samje = evening. raatri = night. ivattu beligge = this morning. naale samje = tomorrow evening.

sentences.gloss: There is a meeting in the evening
sentences.grammar_note: Time of day goes at the front of the sentence. samje meeting ide. beligge coffee ide. raatri oota ide. Same pattern as ivattu and naale.
aamele = after / later. aamele maataadona = let us talk later. aamele hogona = let us go later. Very useful to close a conversation politely.
aaytu with an adjective means it became that. late aaytu = got late. full aaytu = got full. dark aaytu = got dark. It is the became word.
hatra means near or with, but nimma hatra ide = you have it (literally: near you it exists). nanna hatra illa = I don't have it. The possession idiom in Kannada.
No verb needed for statements of fact in present context. eega lunch time. naale holiday. ivattu Friday. The noun is sufficient.
naale beligge = tomorrow morning. Stack time expressions freely. naale samje = tomorrow evening. ninne raatri = last night.

sentences.gloss: Wait a moment, I am coming
sentences.grammar_note: iri = stay / wait (polite command). svalpa iri = wait a bit. iru = the base verb. iddini = I am here. iri = you stay (polite).

sentences.gloss: Where will I find an auto?
sentences.grammar_note: elli sigatte = where will it be found. Works for anything you are searching for. wifi elli sigatte? good coffee elli sigatte? auto elli sigatte?
-inda means from or by. signal-inda straight = from the signal, straight. mane-inda office = from home to office. bus-inda = by bus.
tago = take (casual). tagoli = take (polite). alli right tagoli = take a right there. alli left tagoli = take a left there.
eshtu doora = how far. doora ide = it is far. hatra ide = it is near / close. tumbaa doora alla = not very far.
maadabohudu = can do / is allowed. park maadabohuduaa? = can I park? U-turn maadabohuduaa? = can I U-turn? Any English action word + maadabohuduaa works.
nanna = my. nimma = your (polite). avara = their/his/her (polite). nanna hatra = with me. nimma hatra = with you.

sentences.gloss: Does UPI work? / Will you take UPI?
sentences.grammar_note: aagattaa = will it work / is it ok. Card aagattaa? = does card work? Cash beda, UPI aagattaa? = no cash, is UPI ok? The universal payment question.
-inda with phone or app means via or using. phone-inda = via phone. app-inda = via app. Same -inda as from, extended to mean by means of.
Any noun + beku is a complete, polite request. receipt beku. bill beku. change beku. bag beku. No extra words needed.
svalpa softens any request. svalpa kammi maadi = make it a little less. svalpa discount iddyaa = is there any discount. svalpa iri = wait just a bit.
The shortest price question. idu = this. eshtu = how much. Two words, complete sentence. Works anywhere — market, menu, cab fare.

sentences.gloss: It will be a hundred rupees
sentences.grammar_note: nooru = 100. nooru roopaayi = 100 rupees. aagatte = it will become (the price). eshtu roopaayi aagatte? = how many rupees will it be?
jaasti = more / excess. kammi = less. jaasti aaytu = it became too much. jaasti beda = don't want so much. kammi kodu = give less.
kammi maadi = make it less. Works for price, spice, quantity, volume. plees at the end softens it further. Very natural bargaining phrase.
iddyaa = is there / do you have (question form of ide). change iddyaa = is there change. time iddyaa = is there time. stock iddyaa = is it in stock.
English adjective + aa makes a yes-no question. fresh aa? = is it fresh? good quality aa? = is it good quality? original aa? = is it original?
bere = other or different. bere color = another color. bere size = another size. bere option iddyaa = is there another option. Highly useful word.
nange = to me / for me. nanage is the full form, nange is the spoken shortening. nange beku = I want / need. nange beda = I don't want.

sentences.new_word_meaning: I am taking / I will take
sentences.grammar_note: tagolteeni = I am taking. idu tagolteeni = I'll take this. adu beda, idu tagolteeni = don't want that, taking this.
kodi = give (polite). kodu = give (neutral). nimage kodu = giving to you. bag kodi = give a bag. receipt kodi = give a receipt.

sentences.gloss: How are you, are you doing well?
sentences.new_word_meaning: are you well / are you good (polite)
sentences.grammar_note: chennaagiddeera = are you fine (polite). chennaagiddini = I am fine. The complete greeting exchange in Bangalore Kannada.

sentences.gloss: It has been a long time
sentences.grammar_note: dina = day. tumbaa dina aaytu = many days have become = it has been a long time. eshtu dina aaytu = how many days has it been.

sentences.gloss: What is the scene? / What is going on?
sentences.grammar_note: scene is pure Bangalore slang. scene enu = what's happening. scene illa = not happening / no go. good scene ide = situation is good. Used constantly.
full is a Bangalore intensifier. full busy = totally busy. full traffic ide = completely gridlocked. full tired aagiddini = I am completely tired. Different from English full.
kudi = drink. kudiyona = let us drink. oota maadona = let us eat. samje coffee kudiyona = let us have coffee in the evening.

sentences.new_word_meaning: let us meet / let us find each other
sentences.grammar_note: sigu = meet / find. sigona = let us meet. naale sigona = see you tomorrow. office-alli sigona = let us meet at the office.
mast = awesome, from Hindi mast but fully absorbed into Bangalore Kannada. mast aaytu = that was great. mast ide = it is awesome. The most Bangalore compliment.
maadkobeda = don't do it to yourself (casual). maadkobeedi = don't do it (polite). tension maadkobeda = don't stress. worry maadkobeda = don't worry.
adjust maadkolli is the great Bangalore phrase. It asks someone to make do, be flexible, accommodate a situation. Used when things are not perfect but workable.
namaskaara works for both hello and goodbye. sari, namaskaara = ok, goodbye. The most natural sign-off in Bangalore Kannada. aaytu, namaskaara = done, goodbye.

stages.name: Social Fluency
stages.tagline: Mostly Kannada, English where Kannada has no word
stages.description: Family, health, market, emotions. The conversations that matter beyond navigation. Bangalore-specific words start appearing here — mast, full, scene.

sentences.gloss: There are five people in our house
sentences.grammar_note: jana = person. aidu jana = five people. eshtu jana = how many people. iddaare = they are (polite third person plural). namma = our.
appa = dad. amma = mom. iddaare = he/she is (polite, for elders and respected people). Never use ide for your own parents — use iddaare.
adige = cooking or kitchen. adige maadu = to cook (literally: do the cooking). amma adige maadteeri = mother is cooking (you would say to someone outside the family).

sentences.gloss: Elder brother is working in Bengaluru
sentences.grammar_note: anna = elder brother. akka = elder sister. tamma = younger brother. tangi = younger sister. iddaane = he is (casual, for peers). iddaare = he is (polite, for elders).

sentences.gloss: Elder sister's wedding is next month
sentences.grammar_note: maduve = wedding. tumbaa maduve scene ide Bengalooru-alli = there is a lot of wedding activity in Bangalore. mundina tingalu = next month. hecchina tingalu = previous month.

sentences.gloss: The child is going to school
sentences.grammar_note: magu = small child. makkalu = children. maga = son (older). magaLu = daughter. magu shaale-ge = child to school. eshtu vayassu? = how old?

sentences.gloss: I have come from our hometown
sentences.grammar_note: ooru = hometown or any town. namma ooru = our hometown. ooru-ninda = from hometown. -ninda is the from postposition (same as -inda but joins with n for flow).
English verb + aagiddaare = he/she has become that (polite, for elders). retire aagiddaare = has retired. transfer aagiddaare = has been transferred. promote aagiddaare = has been promoted.

sentences.kannada: ಅಮ್ಮ ಬೆಳಿಗ್ಗೆ ಬೇಗ ಎದ್ದು ಅಡಿಗೆ ಮಾಡ್ತಾರೆ
sentences.roman: amma beligge bega eddu adige maadtaare
sentences.gloss: Mother wakes up early in the morning and cooks
sentences.new_word_meaning: having woken up / getting up (verb connector)
sentences.grammar_note: eddu = having gotten up (connector form of eelu). Two actions connected: eddu adige maadu = wake up and cook. Verb + du/i connects sequential actions.

sentences.kannada: ನಾವು ರಾತ್ರಿ ಎಲ್ಲಾ ಒಟ್ಟಿಗೆ ಊಟ ಮಾಡ್ತೀವಿ
sentences.roman: naavu raatri ellaa ottige oota maadteevi
sentences.gloss: We all eat together at night
sentences.grammar_note: ottige = together. ellaa ottige = all together. naavu = we. maadteevi = we do (first person plural). The -teevi ending is we-present-continuous.
nanage X ide = I have X (literally: to me X exists). nanage jvara ide = I have fever. nanage novu ide = I have pain. The possession idiom applies to ailments too.
novu = pain. tale novu = headache (literally: head pain). hotte novu = stomach ache. kai novu = hand/arm pain. elli novu ide? = where is the pain?

sentences.gloss: Need to go to the doctor
sentences.grammar_note: hog + beku = need to go / must go. maad + beku = need to do. tinn + beku = need to eat. Verb root + beku = need to do that verb. One of the most useful constructions.

sentences.gloss: If you take medicine it will be fine
sentences.grammar_note: maatre = tablet or pill (specifically). ooshadhi = medicine in general. tagondre = if you take. verb + ndre = if you do that. hogondre aagatte = if you go it will work out.

sentences.gloss: The hospital is near from here
sentences.grammar_note: aspatre = hospital. illinda = from here. hatra ide = is near. illinda hatra ide = is near from here. doora illa = is not far (same meaning, different phrasing).

sentences.gloss: There is pain on this side, in the arm
sentences.grammar_note: kade = side or direction. ee kade = this side. aa kade = that side. kade is used for pointing at a body area or a direction. market kade = towards the market.

sentences.gloss: Fever has come down, I am fine now
sentences.grammar_note: ilidu = to come down / to reduce. jvara ilidide = fever has come down. rate ilidide = price has come down. tumbaa ilidide = it has come down a lot.

sentences.gloss: The doctor has said two days rest
sentences.grammar_note: helu = say/tell. heliddaare = has said (polite, for the doctor). heliddini = I have said. heliddira? = have you said?
hotte = stomach. hotte novu = stomach ache. hotte thumba ide = stomach is full. hotte sari illa = stomach is not right (polite way to say upset stomach).

sentences.gloss: Take good care of your health
sentences.grammar_note: aarogya = health. aarogya chennaagide = health is good. noodkolli = take care (literally: see to yourself). noodkobeku = need to take care. A caring send-off.

sentences.gloss: I am very happy / that made me very happy
sentences.grammar_note: khushi aaytu = became happy / felt happy. khushi ide = is happy. tumbaa khushi = very happy. From Hindi/Urdu but completely natural in Bangalore Kannada.
dukha aaytu = became sad. keli = having heard. adu keli = having heard that. Verb + i connects it to the result: heard + sad became.

sentences.gloss: I am feeling a lot of stress
sentences.grammar_note: nanage tension aagide = to me tension is happening. tension aagutte = tension will happen. tension maadkobeda = do not create tension for yourself.
kopa bantu = anger came = he got angry. kopa = anger. avnige = to him (casual). nanage kopa barutte = I will get angry. kopa illade iru = stay without anger = don't be angry.
bhaya = fear. bhaya padteeni = I am feeling afraid. padbeda = don't feel (casual). padbedi = don't feel (polite). bhaya padkobeedi = don't be afraid (to yourself).
aashcharya aaytu = became surprised. idanna nodi = having seen this. -anna is the object marker: idanna = this (as an object). adanna = that. namanna = us.
nijavaagi = truly. nijavaagluu = truly indeed (emphatic). Used when you want to convey sincerity and not just politeness. nijavaagloo mast ide = it is genuinely awesome.
saaku = enough. saaku maadi = make it enough / stop. innu beda = no more. innu = more / still / yet. saaku, innu beda = enough, no more.
innoo = still more / a bit more. innoo beku = want more. innoo illa = still not there. innoo svalpa = a little more. A single word that covers still, yet, and more.

sentences.gloss: It is enough now / I have had enough
sentences.grammar_note: saakagide = has become enough. innu saakagide = now it is enough. Useful for politely declining food, work, a conversation that has run its course.

sentences.gloss: What does that mean? / What do you mean?
sentences.new_word_meaning: meaning / that is to say / so basically
sentences.grammar_note: amdre = meaning or that is to say. adu amdre enu? = what does that mean? amdre naanu hogbeku = meaning I need to go. The single most useful discourse word in Kannada.

sentences.gloss: So you mean you are not coming tomorrow?
sentences.grammar_note: anta = that (as a quote marker). baralla anta? = you are saying won't come? heliddaare anta = they said that. It closes reported speech.

sentences.gloss: Just not that / only that I don't want
sentences.grammar_note: maatra = only or just. adu maatra beda = only that I don't want (everything else is fine). naanu maatra hogteeni = only I am going (not others). Precise, emphatic.
yaaru = who. yaaraadru = anyone / someone. enu + aadru = enaadru = something/anything. elli + aadru = ellaadru = somewhere. Adding aadru makes it open/indefinite.

sentences.gloss: This is different / This is another kind
sentences.grammar_note: tara = type or kind. bere tara = different kind. aa tara = that kind. hege tara = which kind. hege tara maadteeri? = how do you do it? (in what manner)

sentences.gloss: In that case I will go / if so I will go
sentences.new_word_meaning: in that case / if so / then
sentences.grammar_note: haagaadre = if that is so / in that case. haagirabeku = it should be like that. haage = like that / so. haagaadre is the pivot for conditional conclusions.

sentences.gloss: Even so it is ok, I will go
sentences.grammar_note: aadroo = even so / nevertheless. aadre = but/if. aadroo sari = even so it is fine. late aadroo sari = even if it is late, it is fine. Strong acceptance.

sentences.gloss: According to me it is not right
sentences.new_word_meaning: according to / in the opinion of
sentences.grammar_note: nanna prakaara = according to me / in my opinion. nimma prakaara = according to you. avara prakaara = according to them. A polite way to disagree.

sentences.gloss: That is exactly what I am saying
sentences.grammar_note: taane adds emphasis: ade taane = that very thing. naane = I myself. ninage taane = to you only. Used when you want to stress that something is exact.

sentences.gloss: What you said is right, isn't it
sentences.new_word_meaning: what was said / the thing that was said
sentences.grammar_note: verb + iddu = the thing that was done. helliddu = what was said. maadiddu = what was done. neevu helliddu = what you said. A nominalized verb, very common in Kannada.
tarakaari = vegetables (general). tomato, eerulli (onion), aalu (potato) are common. fresh aagide = has become fresh = is fresh. ivattu fresh aagide = today it is fresh.
keji = kilogram. eradu keji = two kilos. aadu keji = half kilo. tooka = weight. tookа maadi = weigh it. keji is the everyday word, not gram or kilo formally.

sentences.gloss: What is the price of onions?
sentences.grammar_note: eerulli = onion (specific Kannada word, used daily). ret = rate/price. eerulli ret jaasti aaytu = onion price has gone up. Common complaint in Bangalore markets.
taaja = fresh (from Hindi/Urdu, used for produce). fresh and taaja both work in Bangalore. taaja haalu = fresh milk. taaja tarkari = fresh vegetables.
tooka = weight. tooka haaku = put/measure the weight. sariyaagi = correctly. tookа sari illa = the weight is not right. Useful when you suspect incorrect weighing.
kattu = bunch or bundle. ondu kattu = one bunch. eradu kattu = two bunches. saasive kattu = bunch of mustard greens. kattu also means to tie or build.
tutti = expensive/costly. cheepu = cheap. tutti aaytu = it has become expensive. eshtu tutti = how expensive. ishtу tutti = this expensive (ishtu = this much).
idna = idanna shortform = this (object). idna exchange maadabohuduaa = can this be exchanged. return maadabohuduaa = can it be returned. The -na object marker shortens in speech to -na.

sentences.gloss: The size is not right, give another
sentences.grammar_note: alate = size or measurement. alate sari illa = size is not right. alate nodi kodu = see the size and give. size and alate are both used in Bangalore shops.
chillare = small change or coins. change is also used but chillare is the Kannada word. chillare illa = no change. chillare kodu = give change.
hesaru = name. nimma hesrenu = what is your name (spoken shortening). nimma hesru enu? is the full form but hesrenu is how it is actually said. nanna hesru ___ = my name is ___.

sentences.new_word_meaning: person from a place / native of
sentences.grammar_note: ooru + inavaru = person from that place. yaava ooru-inavaru = from which place. Bengalooru-inavaru = person from Bengaluru. A friendly question to understand someone's background.

sentences.gloss: I know a little bit of Kannada
sentences.grammar_note: svalpa svalpa = little by little / a tiny bit (repetition softens). gottu = know. nanage kannada svalpa svalpa gottu = I know Kannada little by little. A humble and endearing way to say you are learning.

sentences.new_word_meaning: I am in the process of learning
sentences.grammar_note: kali = learn. kaleetaa iddini = I am learning (ongoing process). maadtaa iddini = I am in the process of doing. The -taa iddini form signals work in progress.
sahaaya = help. sahaaya beku = need help. sahaaya maadi = please help. nimma sahaaya beku = I need your help. A clear, dignified way to ask for help.

sentences.gloss: There was one thing / I had something (to ask)
sentences.new_word_meaning: was there / there was (past of ide)
sentences.grammar_note: ittu = was there (past tense of ide). ondu kelasa ittu = there was one task/thing. A common conversation opener meaning I have something to ask you.

sentences.gloss: I needed to talk to you
sentences.grammar_note: jote = with (for people). nimma jote = with you. avara jote = with them. hatra = near/with (for things). jote = with (for people and activities done together).

sentences.gloss: If you get time come once
sentences.grammar_note: omme = once / one time. eradu omme = twice. sikkidre = if you get/find. samaya sikkidre = if time is found / if you get time. A warm, low-pressure invitation.
kodteera? = will you give? kodu = give. kodteeni = I will give. kodteera = will you give. nimma number kodu = give your number (direct). kodteera = will you give (softer).
matte = again. matte banni = come again. matte maadi = do it again. matte sigona = let us meet again. A warm goodbye that leaves the door open.

sentences.new_word_meaning: did you do / have you done (polite)
sentences.grammar_note: maadidra = did you do / have you done (polite question). maadidini = I did. maadiddaare = he/she has done. Asking if someone has eaten is a standard Kannada greeting.
adige = cooked food or cooking. mane-alli adige ide = there is food at home (meaning cooked food ready). hotel-ge hogbeda = no need to go to a restaurant.

sentences.gloss: There is rice, rasam, and stir-fry
sentences.grammar_note: anna = cooked rice. saaru = rasam (thin spiced soup). palya = dry vegetable stir-fry. The core Karnataka meal. sambaar is the thicker lentil-vegetable curry.
tuppa = ghee. haakla? = shall I put? (haaklaa = haaku + laa, the shall-I form). haakla is a warm hosting question. neeru haakla = shall I pour water?

sentences.gloss: It is enough, it has been a lot
sentences.new_word_meaning: it has become enough / I am full
sentences.grammar_note: saakaaytu = saaku + aaytu = it has become enough. Used to politely decline more food. Stronger than saaku alone. Always appreciated at a host's table.
tinnu = eat (verb). tinni = eat (polite command). tinnbeedi = do not eat (polite). innu svalpa tinni = eat just a little more. The persistent hosting phrase.
sihi = sweet (taste). khaara = spicy. huli = sour. uppu = salty. kaara = bitter. sihi tindi = sweet snack. sihi tindra = did you have sweets? (after a meal).
illade = without (literally: not being there). kaaphi illade = without coffee. neevu illade = without you. illade aagalla = cannot do without it. The dependency construction.

sentences.gloss: Will you give a glass of water?
sentences.grammar_note: lota = glass (for drinking, typically stainless steel). cup is also used. ondu lota neeru = one glass of water. eradu cup coffee = two cups of coffee.

sentences.gloss: The meal was good, thank you
sentences.grammar_note: dhanyavaada = thank you (formal, respectful). thanks is also common. swalpa tondare aaytu = caused you a little trouble (said when leaving). A gracious way to end a meal visit.

sentences.gloss: He is a very good person
sentences.grammar_note: manushya = person (more character-focused than jana which counts people). olle manushya = good person. manasa olle manushya = person with a good heart.
chalaaki = clever or sharp (from Hindi chalak). avalu chalaaki = she is smart. chalaaki maadu = to be clever about something. Often said with admiration.
English adjective + aagiddaare = he/she has been that way (sustained state, polite). helpful aagiddaare. supportive aagiddaare. strict aagiddaare. Very natural way to describe someone.

sentences.new_word_meaning: it comes / he knows / he is capable of (for skills)
sentences.grammar_note: baratte = it comes to him = he knows how to. kannada baratte = he knows Kannada. khaana maadodu baratte = he knows how to cook. The skill possession idiom.

sentences.gloss: I do not know how to swim
sentences.grammar_note: eeju = swimming. eeju baralla = swimming does not come = I cannot swim. drive baratte = knows how to drive. baratte/baralla is the skill can/cannot pair.

sentences.gloss: Need to learn little by little
sentences.grammar_note: kali = to learn. kalibeku = need to learn. kaleetaiddini = I am learning. kalitini = I learned. svalpa svalpa kali = learn little by little. Encouragement for any learning journey.
gottu = know. gottilla = don't know (present). gottirallilla = did not know (past). The past negative adds -irallilla. Long but very common: gottirallilla sari aaytu = I didn't know, fair enough.

sentences.gloss: Now I understand / now I got it
sentences.grammar_note: gottaaytu = it became known = I understood. eega gottaaytu = now I get it. A natural response when something is explained to you. gottu + aaytu = understanding achieved.
matte + omme = mattomme = one more time. mattomme helu = say it again. mattomme maadu = do it again. Polite way to ask for repetition when you did not understand.
nidhaana = slow or careful. nidhaanavaagi = slowly. bega = fast. nidhaanavaagi maataadi = speak slowly please. nidhaanavaagi hogi = go slowly / drive carefully.

sentences.gloss: It looks like rain will come today
sentences.grammar_note: male = rain. male baratte = rain will come. male banthу = rain came. kaanatte = it looks like / it appears. anta kaanatte = it seems that. Weather small talk in Bangalore.

sentences.new_word_meaning: has stopped / has stood (past, present result)
sentences.grammar_note: nillu = to stop/stand. nintide = has stopped (with current relevance). male nintide = rain has stopped (so we can go now). bus nintide = bus has stopped.

sentences.gloss: The sun / heat is very intense
sentences.grammar_note: bisilu = sunlight or sunshine heat. bisi = hot (temperature). bisilu = the sun's heat specifically. bisilu tumbaa ide = it is very hot outside. nimala tagoli = take an umbrella.
chali = cold (weather or feeling). bisilu = hot sun. male = rain. chali tumbaa ide = very cold. chali aagide = it has become cold. sweater haakoli = please wear a sweater.

sentences.gloss: The wind is blowing a lot
sentences.grammar_note: gaali = wind. gaali beesu = to blow (wind). beestide = is blowing. gaali tumbaa beestu ide = the wind is blowing very much. fresh gaali ide = fresh air is there.

sentences.gloss: Day after tomorrow is a holiday
sentences.grammar_note: ivattu = today. naale = tomorrow. naadiddu = day after tomorrow. ninne = yesterday. monnine = day before yesterday. raje = holiday or leave day.

sentences.gloss: There was a lot of work this week
sentences.grammar_note: ee vaara = this week. mundina vaara = next week. hinde vaara = last week. ee vaara tumbaa kelasa ittu = this week there was a lot of work.

sentences.gloss: Salary comes at the end of the month
sentences.grammar_note: sambala = salary. tingala konege = at month end. sambala baratte = salary will come. sambala aaytu = salary has come. The most anticipated sentence of the month.

sentences.gloss: Need to take a small loan
sentences.grammar_note: tagobbekide = need to take (tagо + beku + ide = taking is needed). Combines the need-to construction with ide for a softer, more tentative meaning. Useful for uncertain plans.

sentences.gloss: This month expenses have been a lot
sentences.grammar_note: kharchu = expense (from Urdu). kharchu jaasti aaytu = expenses have gone up. kharchu kammi maadbekuu = need to reduce expenses. ee tingalu kharchu tumbaa aaytu = this month spending was a lot.
badalaaagu = to change. badalaagide = has changed. Bengalooru tumbaa badalaagide = Bengaluru has changed a lot. The standard opening for any conversation about the city's transformation.

sentences.gloss: It was not like this before
sentences.grammar_note: modalu = before or first. modalu heegittu = it used to be like this. modalu heegirallilla = it was not like this before. Often heard in conversations about Bangalore's rapid change.

sentences.gloss: Now there are a lot of people (population has grown)
sentences.grammar_note: jana aagiddaare = people have happened = population has grown. tumbaa jana aagiddaare = so many people have come. This is how Bangaloreans comment on population growth.

sentences.gloss: How many years have you been here?
sentences.grammar_note: varsha = year. eshtu varsha = how many years. varsha-dinda = since years. illiddiri = you have been here (polite). eshtu varsha-dinda Bengalooru-alli iddiri = how many years in Bangalore.

sentences.gloss: It has been ten years since coming
sentences.grammar_note: hattu varsha aaytu bandu = ten years have become since having come. bandu = having come (connector). The pattern: time + aaytu + having-done.

sentences.gloss: Now I am used to it / now it is habit
sentences.grammar_note: abhyaasa aagide = habit has formed = I am used to it. abhyaasa aagilla = not used to it yet. Bengalooru-ge abhyaasa aaytu = got used to Bangalore.
yaavudu = which one. yaava = which (before a noun). nimma ooru yaavudu = your hometown which one is it. nimma ooru yaava = which is your town (before ooru).

sentences.gloss: I am from the Mysuru side
sentences.new_word_meaning: person from that side / from that direction
sentences.grammar_note: kade = direction/side. kadeyavanu = person from that side. Mysuru kadeyavanu = from the Mysore direction/region. A casual way to state your origin, less specific than naming the town.

sentences.gloss: Our Kannada is different in different places
sentences.grammar_note: bere bere = different different = varied. Mysuru kannada, Dharwad kannada, Mangalore kannada are quite distinct. Bengaluru kannada is a blend. This sentence comes up when comparing dialects.

sentences.gloss: But you can understand it right
sentences.grammar_note: artha = meaning or understanding. artha aagatte = meaning will come = you will understand. artha aagalla = cannot understand. adu amdre enu, artha aagalla = what does that mean, I cannot understand.

stages.name: Naturalistic Bangalore
stages.description: How fluent Bangalore speakers actually talk. Code-switching is intentional here, not a gap. You know when to use the English word for effect and when the Kannada word lands better.
